rationale: A0 shows the legitimate Fax service being hijacked via 'sc config' to replace its binPath with a PowerShell command, a classic service-based persistence/execution technique. EV-001 and EV-002 confirm a second service ('[x]_CMD') was created and started pointing to a custom binary at C:\[x]\[x]\[id]\bin\[x].exe, and EV-003 shows PowerShell also registering a '[x]_PowerShell' service pointing to the same binary. EV-004 confirms that binary was successfully launched as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM via services.exe, meaning privilege escalation to SYSTEM was achieved. EV-005 shows the Fax service binPath was subsequently restored to its original value (fxssvc.exe), indicating deliberate cleanup to reduce forensic footprint — a strong indicator of intentional malicious tradecraft rather than accidental misconfiguration. The entire sequence (service hijack, custom service creation, SYSTEM-level execution, cleanup) is consistent with a privilege escalation and persistence attack, likely part of a red-team or adversary simulation framework given the [x]/[id] placeholder naming convention, but the techniques and SYSTEM-level code execution are confirmed malicious regardless.
